name: Sacred Geometry
shortDescription: Sacred geometry attributes symbolic and sacred meanings to certain geometric shapes and proportions. It is often associated with the belief that geometry is a fundamental language of the universe and a reflection of divine order.
description: Sacred geometry explores the philosophical and symbolic significance of geometric shapes and ratios, often connecting them to spiritual or cosmological beliefs. It posits that underlying patterns and mathematical relationships are inherent in the structure of the universe and are reflected in art, architecture, and nature. Specific shapes, such as the circle, triangle, square, pentagon (often through the golden ratio), and platonic solids, are imbued with symbolic meaning and are believed to represent different aspects of creation, divinity, or the cosmos. The concept has been explored across various cultures and time periods, from ancient Egypt and Greece to Renaissance Europe and contemporary New Age movements. It's often associated with the idea that by understanding and utilizing these geometric principles, one can gain insight into the nature of reality and connect with a higher power. The study of sacred geometry often blends mathematics, philosophy, art, and spirituality, offering a framework for understanding the interconnectedness of all things. The golden ratio, for example, is frequently cited as a key element in sacred geometry, appearing in natural forms and artistic compositions, and believed to represent perfect proportion and harmony. The flower of life, a complex geometric pattern composed of overlapping circles, is another commonly cited example, symbolizing creation and the interconnectedness of all life. Many ancient temples and sacred sites are believed to have been designed using sacred geometric principles, reflecting a desire to align earthly structures with cosmic order.

sources.title: The Key to Geometry: Being a new and exact Method, for easily demonstrating all such Propositions as are useful in every Art and Science
sources.author: Henry Coggeshall
